skill.name: test-driven-development
skill.description: Guides feature work, bug fixes, and refactoring through a strict red-green-refactor test-driven development workflow.
skill.summary: Apply test-first development with verified failures, minimal implementation, and safe refactoring.
skill.author: CodingCossack

security_audit.summary: AI review found no prompt injection, executable malware logic, credential access, or data exfiltration. Static detections are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline backticks, and testing terminology. The only confirmed command guidance is local test runner usage such as npm test.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections come from Markdown code fences, inline code, and local test command examples. No executable shell wrapper or dynamic command execution was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are documentation examples and prose. The skill contains no script file or code path that executes backtick content.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections occur in prose and testing examples, not cryptographic API usage. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of each listed line found TDD guidance, method names, or prose. There are no cryptographic operations or unsafe algorithm recommendations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance detection points to a test list bullet, not scanning behavior. No network probing command or discovery workflow was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 237 is ordinary test planning text. The surrounding section lists retry function test cases, with no network scanning intent.

content.value_statement: Teams often add tests after code and miss broken behavior. This skill enforces test-first work with red, green, and refactor checkpoints.
actual_capabilities: Guides the red-green-refactor cycle for behavior changes.
Requires a failing test before new production behavior.
Helps verify that tests fail for the intended reason.
Provides rules for minimal implementation and safe refactoring.
Explains characterization tests for legacy code without coverage.
Identifies common mock, test utility, and interaction-order anti-patterns.
limitations: Does not execute or validate tests by itself.
Assumes the project has a working test runner.
Uses an opinionated workflow that may not fit quick prototypes.
Does not replace domain expertise for complex test design.

use_cases.title: Implement a Feature Safely
use_cases.description: Create one failing test, add the smallest passing code, then refactor while tests stay green.

use_cases.title: Fix a Regression
use_cases.description: Reproduce the bug with a failing test before changing production code, then keep the test as coverage.

use_cases.title: Improve Legacy Code
use_cases.description: Add characterization tests to capture current behavior before refactoring or adding new behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Small Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use test-driven development for this feature: [describe feature]. Start with one failing test and wait for verification before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a Bug with Proof
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply TDD to fix this bug: [describe bug]. First write a test that fails for the current behavior and explains the regression.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Behind Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me refactor [component or module]. Identify existing behavior tests first, add characterization tests if needed, then keep behavior unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Review Test Quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these tests for TDD quality, mock overuse, brittle assertions, and missing behavior coverage. Recommend concrete changes before implementation continues.

output_examples.input: Add retry behavior to a network client.
output: A first failing test that describes retry count and final success.
A minimal implementation plan that only satisfies that test.
A refactor checkpoint after the test passes.

output_examples.input: Fix empty email addresses being accepted.
output: A regression test that fails when empty email is accepted.
A small validation change that makes the test pass.
A recommendation to keep the test as permanent coverage.

output_examples.input: Clean up a legacy service without tests.
output: A characterization test list for current behavior.
A safe refactoring order with tests kept green.
A boundary for new behavior that must use failing tests first.

best_practices: Run the new test and confirm it fails before writing production code.
Assert observable behavior instead of internal implementation details.
Refactor only after tests pass, and keep the full suite green.
anti_patterns: Writing production behavior first and adding tests afterward.
Testing mock existence instead of real user-visible behavior.
Adding test-only methods to production classes for convenience.

faq.question: Does this skill write tests for me?
faq.answer: It guides the workflow and test design. The assistant still needs project context and a working test runner.

faq.question: Can I use it on legacy code?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends characterization tests first, then TDD for new behavior changes.

faq.question: Does every refactor need a new failing test?
faq.answer: No. Pure refactoring keeps behavior unchanged, so existing tests should stay green throughout.

faq.question: What if the test passes immediately?
faq.answer: The test may cover existing behavior or the wrong requirement. Revise it until it fails for the intended reason.

faq.question: Does it require a specific test framework?
faq.answer: No. The guidance works with common test runners, but examples use JavaScript and TypeScript style tests.

faq.question: Is this safe for publication?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found no malicious behavior, prompt injection, or unsafe executable code.
